summary: Gruber pushes back against widespread rumors that the iPad 2 will feature a retina display with 2048×1536 resolution. He acknowledges the circumstantial evidence — double-resolution UI graphics found in iBooks, Engadget's sourced report of a 'super high resolution' screen — but argues the cost of such a display and the required RAM make it prohibitive for 2011. Based on his own sources, he's confident the iPad 2 will keep the 1024×768 resolution, comparing its upgrade trajectory to the iPhone 3GS rather than the iPhone 4. He also argues that any intermediate resolution (like 1280×960) would be unlikely because it would force developers to support three sets of pixel-perfect graphics.
key_points: Double-resolution iPad graphics found in iBooks are likely forward-looking design work, not evidence of imminent hardware
A 2048×1536 display would be cost-prohibitive in 2011, requiring far more RAM than the current iPad's 256 MB — approaching the pixel count of a 27-inch Cinema Display
The iPad's upgrade cycle mirrors the iPhone's: three generations at original resolution before a retina display leap
Any intermediate resolution between 1024×768 and 2048×1536 would saddle developers with three sets of pixel-perfect graphics, making it impractical
Gruber's sources specifically deny a retina display, though he notes the 'double or nothing' claim is his own analysis, not sourced
money_quotes: Long story short: No, it won't.
Trust me, it's double or nothing.
I hope it's true, because it'd be beautiful, but I'll believe it when I see it.
There are 2048 × 1536 iPad prototypes in Cupertino. They're not the iPad 2, though.
Double-resolution graphics created now are double-resolution graphics that don't have to be created under a tight deadline later, when such an iPad actually ships.
Am I certain the iPad 2 remains at 1024 × 768? No. But I'd wager on it — heavily.
It seems perverse to begin speculating about the iPad 3 when we're still months away from the iPad 2.
Supporting multiple resolutions with pixel-perfect UIs is hard.
key_insight: Hardware rumors must be weighed against economic and engineering constraints — a retina iPad display was technically possible but cost-prohibitive, and Apple's pixel-doubling strategy meant anything short of exactly 2048×1536 would create an unsustainable burden for developers.
